core_concept: Market Economy Mechanics: Competition, Prices, Profit, and Innovation
facts: When multiple businesses compete for the same customers, they often lower prices or improve product quality because they must attract buyers away from rivals; this is how competition benefits consumers.
Profit is not the same as revenue; profit equals total revenue minus total costs, so a business with high revenue may still lose money if its costs are even higher.
In a monopoly, there is only one seller in the market, so that seller can set higher prices without fear of losing customers to a competitor, which often leads to lower consumer surplus.

Entrepreneurs are people who start new businesses to provide goods or services, hoping to earn profit; their innovations and entry into markets increase competition and can disrupt existing monopolies.

Market share refers to the percentage of total sales in an industry that a company controls; a company with a very large market share may become complacent and less innovative because it faces little competitive pressure.

Price signals communicate what consumers want: rising prices for a product signal that demand is high relative to supply, encouraging producers to make more of that product, while falling prices signal oversupply or declining demand.
misconceptions: Students may think that high revenue equals high profit, ignoring that costs can exceed revenue and result in a loss.

Students may believe that a monopoly is beneficial because it can produce more efficiently than many small firms, failing to recognize that monopolies can raise prices and reduce consumer choice without competition.
Students may think entrepreneurs are only motivated by greed and do not play a role in innovation or improving market competition.
